scenario: UB-04 facility bill received alongside CMS-1500 physician bill for same ER visit
patient_context: Commercially insured adult; first ER visit ever
key_billing_concept: UB-04 vs CMS-1500 distinction
key_edge_case: Patient receives two separate bills from two different entities for the same visit and thinks they are being billed twice
expected_agent_focus: Explain that hospitals (UB-04) and physicians (CMS-1500) bill separately; verify both are for the same visit date; total them to show true patient responsibility

scenario: Revenue code on UB-04 not recognized by patient
patient_context: Insured adult; first time receiving UB-04
key_billing_concept: Revenue code translation
key_edge_case: Patient sees revenue code 0360 (Operating Room) and 0272 (Medical/Surgical Supplies) with no plain-language description; does not know what department charged them
expected_agent_focus: Translate revenue codes to department names in plain language; explain that UB-04 uses department codes instead of service descriptions that most patients expect

scenario: Patient frightened by ICD-10 diagnosis code on EOB
patient_context: Insured adult; received EOB with unfamiliar diagnosis code
key_billing_concept: ICD-10 code patient communication
key_edge_case: Patient sees K80.20 on their EOB and fears it means something serious; agent must explain billing-code context without providing a clinical interpretation
expected_agent_focus: Translate ICD-10 code to plain-language billing description (e.g., 'gallstones without current inflammation'); explain this is used for billing/insurance purposes; strongly recommend discussing clinical meaning with their physician — not the agent's role

scenario: HCPCS supply code on itemized bill — patient does not recognize the item
key_billing_concept: HCPCS Level II supply code translation
key_edge_case: Patient sees HCPCS code A6216 (gauze dressing) and J0696 (Ceftriaxone injection) with cryptic descriptions and asks what they were charged for
expected_agent_focus: Translate HCPCS supply/drug codes to plain descriptions (gauze dressing, antibiotic injection); explain these represent supplies and medications used during care; note quantity and unit charge

scenario: CPT code for procedure patient does not remember consenting to
key_billing_concept: CPT code patient recognition
key_edge_case: Patient sees CPT 64483 (paravertebral nerve block injection) on bill and says they were never told this would happen; agent must explain the code without validating or dismissing the patient's concern
expected_agent_focus: Translate CPT code to plain procedure description; explain this is what the provider billed; recommend patient call billing/provider to confirm services performed match surgical consent documentation — do not take a position on whether it was appropriate

scenario: Unknown CPT code not in chargemaster or knowledge docs
key_billing_concept: Agent knowledge boundary for unknown CPT
key_edge_case: Line item contains CPT code 0505T (a newer temporary code for a rare procedure) that is not in the knowledge base; agent cannot verify what it represents
expected_agent_focus: Explicitly state the code could not be verified in available references; avoid inventing a description; recommend patient ask billing department for a plain-language explanation and itemized bill; do not hallucinate

scenario: Missing CPT code — description only on line item
key_billing_concept: Incomplete line item — code absent
key_edge_case: A line item has a description ('LAPAROSCOPIC PROCEDURE') but the CPT code field is blank; agent cannot cross-reference a missing code
expected_agent_focus: Explain based on description text alone; note that the missing code limits the agent's ability to verify the charge; recommend requesting an itemized bill with complete codes from billing; do not guess the CPT code

scenario: Modifier -25 makes patient think E&M visit is a duplicate charge
patient_context: Commercially insured adult; office visit + minor procedure same day
key_billing_concept: CPT Modifier -25 (significant separate E&M)
key_edge_case: Bill shows 99213-25 (office visit) AND 11200 (skin tag removal) on the same date; patient thinks the office visit was billed twice
expected_agent_focus: Explain that modifier -25 means the visit was a distinct, significant evaluation separate from the procedure — not a duplicate; the patient is being charged for two separate services that happened on the same day, which is legitimate when properly documented

scenario: Modifier -50 bilateral procedure makes patient think they were charged twice
key_billing_concept: CPT Modifier -50 (bilateral)
key_edge_case: CMS-1500 shows CPT 29881-50 with quantity 2 and double the unit charge; patient says 'I only had one surgery'
expected_agent_focus: Explain that -50 means the procedure was performed on both sides (e.g., both knees); quantity 2 reflects bilateral billing, not a duplicate; total charge is approximately double a single-side procedure, which is expected

scenario: Modifier -59 flags make patient suspect unbundling or overcharging
key_billing_concept: CPT Modifier -59 (distinct procedural service)
key_edge_case: Bill shows multiple CPT codes with -59 modifiers (e.g., 27447-59, 27370-59); patient has read online that -59 modifiers can sometimes indicate improper unbundling
expected_agent_focus: Explain that -59 indicates each code was a genuinely distinct service, not a bundled component — it is often correct; however, patient concern is valid to raise with billing; recommend requesting an itemized bill with documentation and asking provider to confirm each code was separately performed

scenario: Inpatient DRG bill has one large charge instead of itemized services
key_billing_concept: DRG (Diagnosis Related Group) bundled inpatient billing
key_edge_case: Patient expected to see every IV bag, every nurse visit, every test itemized; sees one DRG line for $42,000 and does not understand the lump-sum billing
expected_agent_focus: Explain that Medicare and many commercial plans pay hospitals a fixed DRG rate for the entire admission — this bundles all services into one payment; the $42,000 is what Medicare/insurance agreed to pay the hospital for this type of case, not a per-service tally; patient responsibility comes from deductible/coinsurance applied to the DRG rate, not each individual item

scenario: Medicare patient billed as outpatient observation after 3-night hospital stay
patient_context: Medicare patient age 68; 3-night stay; billed as observation (not admitted)
key_billing_concept: Observation status vs inpatient admission — Medicare cost difference
key_edge_case: Patient was in the hospital for 3 nights but was never formally 'admitted' as inpatient; billed under Medicare Part B outpatient instead of Part A inpatient; patient owes 20% of all services with no cap, and the stay does not count toward the 3-day qualifying stay required for SNF coverage
expected_agent_focus: Explain the critical observation vs inpatient distinction carefully: outpatient observation means Medicare Part A does not apply; patient owes 20% coinsurance on all Part B services with no out-of-pocket cap; this also affects any future skilled nursing facility (SNF) benefits; recommend asking provider if inpatient admission was appropriate and whether a review/appeal is possible

scenario: ACA-covered preventive screening billed with diagnostic code — unexpected patient cost
key_billing_concept: Preventive vs diagnostic coding — ACA cost-sharing rules
key_edge_case: Patient scheduled a routine colonoscopy expecting $0 cost under ACA; physician removed a polyp during the procedure and added a diagnostic code; insurer reclassified the visit as diagnostic and applied deductible
expected_agent_focus: Explain that adding a diagnostic code or performing a therapeutic action (polyp removal) during a preventive screen can change the billing classification under many plans; ACA preventive protections apply to preventive-coded services — when a therapeutic service is added, cost-sharing rules may apply; recommend patient contact insurer to ask if appeal is possible and whether the coding is correct

scenario: Surgical bill with separately coded components that should be bundled
key_billing_concept: CCI (Correct Coding Initiative) bundling — unbundled charges
key_edge_case: Bill shows CPT 27447 (total knee replacement) PLUS separately billed codes for wound closure (12031) and anesthesia check (99100) that are normally considered part of the surgical package
expected_agent_focus: Flag that some charges may overlap with standard surgical bundling; explain that CMS Correct Coding Initiative (CCI) defines which codes must be bundled together; recommend patient ask billing to confirm all codes comply with CCI edits and are not unbundled components of the main procedure

scenario: Office visit billed as hospital outpatient — different cost-sharing than patient expected
patient_context: Commercially insured adult; visit to hospital-owned physician group
key_billing_concept: Place of Service (POS) code — facility vs non-facility billing
key_edge_case: Patient visited a doctor's office physically but it is owned by the hospital; billed as POS 22 (hospital outpatient) instead of POS 11 (office); patient's plan has a higher facility copay ($60) vs office copay ($25) — patient got a $60 bill when they expected $25
expected_agent_focus: Explain that hospital-owned clinics may bill using hospital facility codes (POS 22), which typically trigger higher cost-sharing under many plans; recommend patient confirm with insurer if the billing location qualifies for the office vs facility tier, and whether an appeal/reclassification is possible

scenario: Medicare patient billed for service after signing ABN — did not understand what they were signing
patient_context: Medicare patient; signed ABN at point of care
key_billing_concept: Advance Beneficiary Notice (ABN) — waiver of Medicare liability
key_edge_case: Patient signed an ABN before a bone density scan; Medicare denied the claim as not medically necessary; patient now owes full charge and says they did not understand the ABN meant they would be responsible if Medicare denied
expected_agent_focus: Explain what an ABN is: it is a formal notice that the provider believes Medicare may deny the claim and asks if the patient agrees to be responsible if denied; by signing, the patient accepted financial responsibility; recommend patient review the ABN they signed, verify the denial reason, and consult Medicare or a patient advocate about appeal options

scenario: Patient has primary and secondary insurance but secondary was not billed — patient owes full balance
patient_context: Patient with PPO primary + Medicare secondary (MSSP)
key_billing_concept: Coordination of Benefits (COB) — primary/secondary sequencing
key_edge_case: Primary PPO paid its share; secondary Medicare was not billed by the provider; patient is left with a $3,200 balance that Medicare may have covered
expected_agent_focus: Explain coordination of benefits: secondary insurance should be billed after primary adjudication; the provider should submit to Medicare after receiving the primary EOB; recommend patient call billing to confirm secondary insurance was billed, and provide the Medicare ID number if not already on file

scenario: Patient billed for complication classified as a hospital-acquired condition (HAC) / never event
key_billing_concept: Hospital-Acquired Condition (HAC) / Never Event — non-billable complication
key_edge_case: Patient develops a surgical site infection post-op; hospital bills for additional treatment days; Medicare and many commercial plans prohibit additional payment for preventable hospital-acquired conditions
expected_agent_focus: Explain that Medicare and many insurers do not pay for certain hospital-acquired conditions (infections, falls, wrong-site surgery) classified as preventable; if the additional charges are related to a HAC, the patient may not owe them; recommend patient ask billing to confirm whether the additional charges are tied to a HAC designation, and whether they comply with CMS non-payment policy

scenario: Patient in skilled nursing facility receives separate bill from outside lab they thought was covered
key_billing_concept: SNF consolidated billing — outside services included in SNF rate
key_edge_case: Patient is in SNF under Medicare Part A; receives a separate bill from a reference lab for blood tests; under consolidated billing, Part A SNF rate must cover most services including labs — the SNF should be paying the lab, not billing the patient
expected_agent_focus: Explain that during a Medicare Part A SNF stay, the SNF receives a consolidated per-diem payment that covers most services including labs and radiology; the outside lab should be billing the SNF, not the patient; recommend patient contact the SNF billing department to have the lab bill redirected, and confirm the Part A stay is still active

scenario: Patient sees Cedars chargemaster online, compares to their bill, and thinks they were overcharged
key_billing_concept: Chargemaster vs negotiated rate vs patient responsibility
key_edge_case: Patient looked up the procedure on the Cedars website price transparency tool and saw a list price of $8,500; their bill shows $12,000 for the same CPT code and they believe they were overcharged
expected_agent_focus: Explain that chargemaster prices are list prices before any insurance contract adjustments; the $12,000 gross charge on the bill should be reduced by the contractual adjustment before the patient owes anything; the final patient responsibility (not the gross charge) is the relevant number; if the contractual adjustment was applied correctly, the patient's net balance should reflect the negotiated rate; recommend comparing the EOB to the patient statement to verify the adjustment was applied
